segments.s200-g89: چنین است = 'such is' or 'thus is' with است as the copula after the adverb چنین; رسم جهان جهان is a noun phrase 'the custom/way of the world' where the doubled جهان is a poetic repetition for emphasis. Word order is typical Persian (predicate then noun phrase) with the copula linking the adverb to the noun phrase.
segments.s200-g90: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'; کردار خویش = 'its own deed(s)' where خوی + suffix ش together give the possessive sense 'own'; از تو دارد نهان is literally 'has hidden from you' — دارد (present of داشتن) combines with the adjective نهان to express 'keeps hidden' (i.e. 'has [it] hidden from you'). Word order is relative clause explaining رسم جهان.
segments.s200-g91: همی is a Durative/emphatic adverb 'continually/always' placed before the verb phrase; با تو = 'with you'; در پرده = 'in/behind a curtain/veil' (= secretly); بازی کند = 'plays/acts (a game/trick)' with کند as the present-tense verb 'does/plays'. The clause describes a repeated or habitual action.
segments.s200-g92: ز بیرون = 'from/outside' or 'outwardly'; ترا is the object form 'to/for you' (ت = 2nd-person suffix plus را as the object/definite marker), and بی‌نیازی is a noun/adjective 'self-sufficiency' or 'appearance of being without need'; کند 'makes' is the verb causing that state. Together: 'it makes you, to outside observers, appear self-sufficient.'
segments.s200-g93: ز باد آمدی = 'you came from the wind' with آمدی past 2nd-person; رفت خواهی is a poetic inversion of خواهی رفت ('you will go'), where the past-stem رفت precedes the future auxiliary خواهی for meter; به گرد here means 'to dust' (گرد = dust) so the clause says you will return to dust. The unusual verb order is poetic but understandable as future meaning.
segments.s200-g94: چه دانی = rhetorical 'what do you know' (how could you know); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; با تو چه خواهند کرد = 'what will they do with/unto you' where خواهند (آینده, 3rd plural) + کرد (do) form the future tense. The clause questions the addressee's knowledge about others' intentions.
segments.s200-g95: ببند is an imperative 'close/fasten' (addressing) or a hortative poetic command; درازیم is first-person plural 'we are long/lie stretched' or 'we lie extended'; و links the clauses; در چنگ آز = 'in the grip/clutch (چنگ) of greed (آز)' — a figurative prepositional phrase placing the subject under greed's hold.
segments.s200-g96: ندانیم = first-person plural negative 'we do not know' or 'we do not (so)'; باز آشکارا = adverbial 'again/anymore openly' (باز here contributes the sense of 'again/anymore' or 'openly' in combination with آشکارا); ز راز = 'of/from the secret' (ز + راز). The clause means 'we do not again openly make the secret known' — i.e., we no longer reveal the secret openly because of greed.
segments.s200-g65: چنین آمد is a literary inversion where the adverb حال (چنین, “thus/so”) precedes آمد (past tense of آمدن, “came/arrived”), giving a poetic ‘thus came’. این گنبد تیزگرد is the subject phrase following the verb (typical of older Persian style): این (this) + گنبد (noun, “vault/dome”) modified by the compound adjective تیزگرد literally “sharp-/swift-turning,” a poetic descriptor formed by تیز + گرد.
segments.s200-g66: گهی ... گاه is a paired adverbial construction meaning ‘now and then / at times’; دهد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form of دادن used in literary Persian as a habitual/present-tense ‘gives/causes’. The nouns شادمانی and درد are direct objects (what it bestows): ‘sometimes it bestows joy, at other times pain.’ Word order is adverb + object + verb + adverb + object, which is natural in verse.
segments.s200-g67: سواران توران is a genitival/noun phrase: سواران (horsemen/riders) + توران (Turan, attributive) meaning ‘the horsemen of Turan’. پس پشت طوس is a compact locative phrase: پس (here ‘behind/after’) + پشت (back/rear) + طوس (proper name Tus), so ‘behind Tus.’ There is no copula — a standard omission in Persian verse.
segments.s200-g68: دلان پر ز کین is an adjectival phrase where پر (here ‘full’) + ز (poetic preposition ‘of/from’) takes کین as its complement: ‘hearts full of hatred/vengeance.’ The coordination و links a parallel phrase سران پر فسوس (leaders/chiefs full of فسوس); سران is plural for leaders/heads and فسوس here means whispering/insidious counsel. The structure is symmetrical and idiomatic: [group] پر ز [thing].
segments.s200-g69: همی indicates continuous or repeated action (‘kept on/continually’), and بارید is the past tense (‘rained/fell’). گرز is the subject (‘maces’). گویی here is a simile-marker meaning ‘as if / as though’ (a literary use not listed in the supplied senses), followed by ز ابر ‘from a cloud’. The whole is ‘maces kept raining, as if from a cloud.’
segments.s200-g70: پس پشت functions adverbially as ‘from the rear / upon the back’; بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’ governing a coordinated list جوشن و خود و گبر (‘armor / the man himself / the gabr’). جوشن is the cuirass/armor, خود = the person himself/body, and گبر is an epithet for an unbeliever/Zoroastrian figure. The sequence shows targets of the falling maces.
segments.s200-g71: نبد is an archaic negative-existential verb meaning ‘there was not / did not exist’; with کس (‘anyone/person’) it yields ‘there was no one’. برزم here functions as an old or poetic locative related to the battlefield/field (sense not listed in the supplied options), اندرون means ‘within/inside’, and پایدار is a predicate adjective ‘steady/steadfast.’ Together: ‘There was no one within the field who stood firm.’
segments.s200-g72: همه is an inclusive quantifier (‘all/every’). کوه is ‘mountain’; کردند is the past-plural of کردن (‘they made’). گردان here is used adjectivally as ‘encircling/turning’ (poetic: ‘forming a ring’), modifying حصار (‘rampart/fortress’). The clause means they turned every mountain into an encircling fortification.
segments.s200-g49: This is a nominal phrase + verb: همه دشت (the whole plain) is the direct object, از ایرانیان کشته is a passive/agent phrase meaning ‘killed by the Iranians’ (agent introduced by از in poetic register), and دید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular 'he saw'. The implicit subject (‘he’) is omitted as is normal in Persian; word order places the object and modifiers before the verb.
segments.s200-g50: This clause strings several pre‑verbal modifiers before the past tense verb دید. سر بخت is a compound meaning ‘the head/top of Fate’ (سر = head/top, بخت = fate/fortune), بیدار and برگشته are predicative/adjectival participles (‘awake’, ‘turned/returned’) describing that state of Fate, and دید again is the past 'he saw'. The sequence implies ‘he saw that Fortune’s head had turned and was awake.’
segments.s200-g51: A nominal phrase with adjectives: دریده is a past participle 'torn' modifying درفش 'banner'; و links to the next noun phrase; نگونسار is an adjective 'mournful/woeful' modifying کوس 'war‑drum'. Poetic word order places modifiers before the nouns and the copula/verb is omitted, so these phrases describe the state of the objects.
segments.s200-g52: A noun phrase: رخ 'face/appearance' with زندگان 'of the living' (genitive relationship expressed without an explicit ezāfe in this poetic line), followed by the adjective تیره 'dark/ashen'. چون introduces a simile 'like' and آبنوس supplies the comparison ('ebony' = very dark). The whole reads 'the faces of the living dark like ebony.'
segments.s200-g53: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed (general)'. نگه کرد is a verbal expression where نگه (gaze/watch) combines with کرد (to do) to mean 'he looked' or 'he cast his gaze'. و links to the next clause; گردان ندید is 'he saw no troop' (گردان = troop, ندید = did not see), with negation built into ندید (past negative).
segments.s200-g54: This continues the negation: ز لشکر = 'from the host/of the army' (poetic preposition ز = از), دلیران و مردان enumerates 'the brave and the men', and ندید repeats the past negative 'he did not see' — i.e. he found none of those forces present. Word order is pre‑verbal object phrases followed by the verb ندید.
segments.s200-g55: همه رزمگه سربسر is a circumstantial phrase meaning 'throughout the whole battlefield' (همه = entire/whole, رزمگه = battlefield, سربسر = everywhere/throughout). کشته بود is a compound past (passive/stative) meaning 'had been killed' or 'were killed' (بود is the past auxiliary). The overall sense is that everywhere on the field people lay slain.
segments.s200-g56: تنانشان is 'their bodies' (تنان = bodies, ـشان = their). بخون here is a poetic contraction of به خون 'with blood' (i.e. 'in blood'); this specific lexical sense is not listed in the provided senses, so it is marked -1. اندر آغشته بود is a predicate: اندر = 'in/within', آغشته = 'soaked/tinged', بود = past 'was' (auxiliary), giving 'their bodies were soaked in blood.'
segments.s200-g121: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. شاه دلیر is a noun + adjective phrase ('king' + 'brave'); in poetry the adjective follows the noun without an explicit ezāfe. این سخنها شنید places the verb شنید (past 'heard') at the end, so the whole clause reads 'when the brave king heard these words.'
segments.s200-g122: بجوشید is a past tense, 3rd-person singular poetic form meaning 'he boiled/was seething' (emotionally). وز here functions as an older/poetic prepositional/qualifying element meaning 'by/with' or 'under the burden of' (hence rendered 'from' or 'by the sorrow'); دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his), and بردمید is a past transitive verb meaning 'took away / lifted off'. The clause as a whole says his emotion boiled up and the sorrow was taken away from his heart.
segments.s200-g123: ز کار برادر is a prepositional/genitive phrase: ز (poetic 'from/because of') + کار ('affair/matter') + برادر ('brother'), so 'because of his brother's matter.' پر از درد بود is a predicative phrase where پر (full) + از (of) + درد (sorrow) is linked by بود (past 'was'), giving 'was full of sorrow.'
segments.s200-g124: بران is a deictic/temporal adverb meaning 'thereupon' or 'on that.' درد بر درد is a repetitive, poetic piling-up 'sorrow upon sorrow' (درد followed by the preposition بر + درد). لشکر فزود has لشکر (the army/host) as subject and فزود (past 'increased') as verb, so the line says that, upon that sorrow, sorrow piled up and the army grew/swollen.
segments.s200-g125: زبان کرد is an idiom 'he put forth his tongue' = 'he spoke' (زبان 'speech/utterance' + کرد as verbal auxiliary). گویا is an adverb meaning 'apparently / as if' introducing reported or emphatic speech; بنفرین is an imperative curse 'Be accursed!' and طوس is the name Tus (here the person). Together the segment reports that he uttered what amounted to a curse on Tus.
segments.s200-g126: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night.' تا is the temporal preposition 'until' that introduces the limiting time, گاه 'time/occasion' is the noun governed by تا, and بانگ خروس is a genitive phrase 'the cry of the rooster' (بانگ + خروس). The whole phrase sets the duration: 'the dark night lasted until the rooster's cry.'
segments.s200-g57: This line contains two short clauses joined by juxtaposition: 'پسر بی‌پدر شد' (the son became fatherless) and 'پدر بی‌پسر' (the father [became] sonless). شد is the past-tense verb 'became' and applies to the first clause; the second clause drops the verb by ellipsis (a common poetic device) and is understood in the same way. Word order is subject + predicate (noun phrase + شد).
segments.s200-g58: The noun phrase 'همه لشگر' ('the whole army') is followed by adjectives/phrases describing its condition: 'گشن' (hungry) and the idiomatic pair 'زیر و زبر' meaning 'upside-down' or 'in disorder'. There is no explicit copula; the state is asserted directly (typical in Persian poetry). 'زیر و زبر' is a fixed binomial where 'زیر' and 'زبر' literally contrast 'under'/'above' but together mean 'topsy‑turvy'.
segments.s200-g59: 'به بیچارگی' is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in misery / in their wretchedness'. 'روی برگاشتند' is a verbal phrase: روی (face) + برگاشتند (they turned/they returned) — literally 'they turned their faces' (i.e. they turned away or returned). برگاشتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural.
segments.s200-g60: This clause lists what they did after turning back: 'سراپرده و خیمه' (inner curtains/inner tents and tents) joined by و 'and', followed by the past plural verb 'بگذاشتند' (they placed/they put up). In context 'بگذاشتند' means 'they pitched/erected' (tents, curtains). Word order is noun(s) + verb.
segments.s200-g61: This clause uses the repetitive negation pattern نه ... نه ... to list things that were missing: 'neither ... nor ...'. Word order is straightforward: nouns joined by و (and) inside each negative. In older poetic Persian this is a common way to enumerate absences.
segments.s200-g62: همه is the subject ('all' / 'everyone'). The predicate is nominal rather than a full verbal clause: میسره functions here as a (poetic/archaic) noun/adjective meaning something like 'means/provisions' or 'resources' (the exact classical sense is uncertain), followed by two adjectival descriptors خسته ('weary') and میمنه. In this line میمنه most likely describes location/aspect ('on the right' / 'right-hand side' — i.e., the right bank), so the whole nominal phrase characterizes the state/condition of the people or their camp.
segments.s200-g63: ازین گونه is an adverbial phrase ('in this manner' / 'thus'); از + این (here written ین as an orthographic variant) + گونه ('manner'). لشکر (the army/host) is the subject, followed by سوی + place to indicate direction ('toward Kaseh-rud'). The verb comes after this noun phrase, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s200-g64: برفتند is an old/poetic past-tense verb meaning 'they went/departed' (بر- prefix + رفتند). بی‌مایه is an adjective meaning 'without means / penniless' (bi- prefix = without + māyeh). تار و پود is a fixed collocation ('warp and weft') used idiomatically to mean cloth/fabric or, by extension, the basic garments — here emphasizing that they left with nothing.
segments.s200-g25: 'بیامد' is simple past 3rd-singular 'he came'. The prepositional/compound element 'باسپ' (here preposed) indicates arrival mounted/on a horse (poetic word order places it before the verb). 'اندر آورد پای' is a small clause where 'آورد' (bring, causative) plus the object 'پای' ('foot') literally 'brought his foot in' = 'set foot inside'; the verb is in past tense and governs the following noun.
segments.s200-g26: This whole phrase characterizes the manner of his coming: 'بکردار' is an adjectival phrase 'of stately/well‑formed bearing' and 'باد' is a poetic/optative or archaic particle often used to mark manner or wish (here functioning to produce the idiomatic 'in stately bearing'). 'اندر آمد ز جای' is literal 'came in/from his place' with 'اندر' = 'in', 'آمد' past 'came', and 'ز جای' = 'from the place.' The order is poetic (adjective + particle before the verbal clause).
segments.s200-g27: 'بپرده‌سرای' is a prefixed locative 'ب-' + 'پرده‌سرای' meaning 'into the inner chamber/inner apartment' (movement into). 'سپهبد' is the title 'spahbed, commander.' 'رسید' is past tense 'arrived/reached' (3rd pers. sg.). The direct object/location precedes the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s200-g28: The phrase 'ز گرد سپه' means 'from the dust/cloud raised by the army' where 'ز' = 'from', 'گرد' here = 'dust' (the dust cloud), and 'سپه' = 'host/army'. 'آسمان تیره دید' is 'he saw the sky dark' — 'آسمان' (sky) + adjective 'تیره' (dark/ominous) with the verb 'دید' (saw) in past tense; word order is adjective before verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s200-g29: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him). What follows is reported speech: 'برخیز' is the imperative 'stand up/arise'. 'کامد سپاه' is an archaic present/presentive 'the army is coming' (کامد = 'comes/approaches'), placed after the imperative in the same line — a compact poetic way to report the warning.
segments.s200-g30: 'یکی گرد برخاست' describes a single cloud/whirl of dust rising: 'یکی' = 'one/a certain', 'گرد' = 'dust/cloud' (here the dust raised by movement), and 'برخاست' is past 'rose up'. 'ز اوردگاه' = 'from the place of arrival/assembly' with 'ز' = 'from' and 'اوردگاه' the place noun; word order places the origin after the verb as is idiomatic in poetry.
segments.s200-g31: 'وزان' = 'from that (place/time)' introduces movement away from the previously mentioned spot. 'جایگه' = 'place/spot'; 'رفت' is past 'he went'; 'نزد پدر' = 'to/near his father' where 'نزد' denotes proximity or presence. The clause is straightforward past narrative order.
segments.s200-g32: 'به چنگ اندرون' = 'into (his) hand' or 'into the grasp' with 'به' = 'to/into' and 'چنگ' = 'grip/hand' (here 'hand' or 'grasp'). 'اندرون' = 'inside/into'. 'گرزه گاو سر' is a compound noun phrase 'the mace (گرزه) [with a] bull's head' — 'گاو سر' describes the mace's head ornament; the whole phrase means he placed the bull‑headed mace into his father's hand. Word order in the noun phrase is noun + qualifying noun (typical in older Persian compounds).
segments.s200-g97: دو بهره ز ایرانیان کشته بود is ordered with the numeral + noun first (دو بهره = 'two [of them]'), ز introduces the source/possessive ('of/from the Iranians'), and کشته بود is a past perfect/passive construction meaning 'had been killed' (بود marking past). The agent is implicit; the line reports that two Iranians were dead.
segments.s200-g98: دگر خسته از رزم برگشته بود places the determiner/pronoun دگر ('the other' / 'another') before the predicate; خسته 'tired' is a predicative adjective, از رزم gives the cause ('from battle'), and برگشته بود is the past perfect of برگشتن ('had returned').
segments.s200-g99: سپهبد ز پیکار دیوانه گشت has the subject سپهبد (a title) first, then the causal z-phrase ز پیکار ('from/because of the fighting'), and the result phrase دیوانه گشت where گشت is the verb 'became' (intransitive/predicative) yielding 'became mad'.
segments.s200-g100: دلش با خرد همچو بیگانه گشت: دلش is دل + pronominal suffix ش 'his heart' as the subject; با خرد ('with/relative to wisdom') frames the domain to which the heart is now estranged; همچو ('like/as') + بیگانه ('stranger/alien') form a predicative simile and گشت again is 'became'.
segments.s200-g101: بلشکرگه اندر می‌و خوان و بزم: بلشکرگه ('army‑camp') is followed by اندر ('in/within'), then a triad naming pleasures (می‌و, خوان, بزم). The list is coordinated by و; this is a locative circumstantial phrase meaning 'in the camp, among wine, the board and feasting.'
segments.s200-g102: سپاه آرزو کرد بر جای رزم: سپاه is the subject ('the army/host'), آرزو is a noun 'wish/desire' that combines with کرد (compound verbal phrase 'made/desired'), and بر جای رزم is a prepositional phrase 'to/on the place of battle' expressing what they wished for (i.e. to return to the battlefield).
segments.s200-g103: جهاندیده گودرز با پیر سر: جهاندیده is an epithet 'world‑wise', followed by the proper name گودرز; با introduces the accompanying attribute پیر سر ('old/hoary head') — here 'با' signals possession/accompaniment rather than instrument.
segments.s200-g104: نه پور و نبیره نه بوم و نه بر is a classic negative pairing using نه ... نه ... 'neither ... nor ...'; پور and نبیره enumerate male descendants ('son' and 'offspring/heir'), and بوم denotes 'native land/homeland'. The final بر here is a nominal meaning 'kin/relative' in older usage (not the preposition), so the phrase says he had no sons, no scions, no homeland and no kin.
segments.s200-g105: The pair نه ... نه ... gives a correlative 'neither ... nor ...'. آن خستگان را خورش is a noun phrase 'that/such food for the weary' with را marking خورش as definite/focused object, and پزشک is the second item of the correlative pair ('nor a physician / nor any healer').
segments.s200-g106: همه جای is a locative 'in every place / everywhere'; غم بود is the main clause 'there was sorrow' with بود in the past. The conjunction و connects an elliptical continuation: خونین سرشک ('blood‑stained teardrop(s)') is a noun phrase whose verb ('was' / 'there were') is understood from the previous بود.
segments.s200-g107: جهاندیدگان is a plural noun 'the experienced/worldly‑wise ones'. پیش + اوی means 'in his presence / before him' (اوی is an archaic third‑person enclitic pronoun). آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came'.
segments.s200-g108: Two descriptive nominal compounds are coordinated: شکسته‌دل 'broken‑hearted' and راه‌جوی 'seeker/wayfarer'. The verb آمدند repeats to agree with these predicates: 'they came (broken‑hearted and seeking a road)'.
segments.s200-g109: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one', دیدبان 'watchman/lookout'. بر سر کوه is a locative phrase 'on the mountain top', and کرد here is the past tense verb 'did/placed/made' used to mean he stationed or set himself there (a common poetic usage).
segments.s200-g110: کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where' (introducing an indirect question or purpose: 'to see where'). دیدگان ('the eyes') is the thing acted on, سوی انبوه 'toward the mass/crowd' gives direction, and کرد again is 'he turned / set (his eyes)'. Literarily: 'he turned his eyes toward the mass to see where (they were/onward)'.
segments.s200-g111: طلایه فرستاد is 'he sent a vanguard / scouts' (فرستاد past of 'to send'). بر هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'to/ toward every direction' (بر + هر + سویی giving the sense 'in every direction').
segments.s200-g112: مگر here introduces the purposive/hopeful clause 'so that / in hopes that' (classical usage), and یابد is a subjunctive/optative third‑person form 'he may find' or 'might find'. آن درد را دارویی is 'a remedy for that pain/affliction', with را marking the definite object (درد) and دارویی 'remedy/medicinal (means)'.
segments.s200-g17: بخیمه درون is a locative phrase (ب + خیمه with poetic order) meaning “inside the tent.” گیو is the proper name and بیدار is a predicate adjective (‘awake’) linked to the subject by the past copula بود (here past tense ‘was’). Word order is topical locative → subject → predicate.
segments.s200-g18: سپهدار گودرز is an appositive/title + name construction (‘the commander Goudarz’). هشیار is a predicate adjective ‘alert’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’ forming the clause ‘Goudarz was alert.’ The title precedes the name as a single noun phrase.
segments.s200-g19: خروش آمد is a light-verbal phrase meaning ‘a roar arose’ (آمد used in the intransitive sense ‘arose/happened’). و joins the next nominal phrase; بانگ زخم تبر is a genitive/compound expression ‘the cry (or sound) of the axe’s wound’ (زخم modifying بانگ), with the noun-noun relation typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s200-g20: سراسیمه شد is ‘became flustered/frantic’ (شد as the copular verb ‘became’). The following proper name گیو is the subject, and پرخاشخر is a predicative adjective/appositive meaning ‘aggressive’ applied to him. The sentence shows poetic inversion: predicate phrase before the explicit subject.
segments.s200-g21: ستاده is a past participle/adjective ‘standing’ that modifies the following noun ابر (‘cloud’), so literally ‘a standing cloud.’ پیش is the preposition ‘before/in front of,’ and پرده‌سرای is the place (‘curtain-hall’ or ‘stage/scene’). The phrase is a compact, poetic locative description.
segments.s200-g22: یکی اسپ is ‘a certain horse/one steed’ (یکی as an indefinite determiner). برگستوان‌ور is an attributive adjective ‘mighty/strong.’ به پای is an adverbial locative phrase ‘at the foot’ or ‘by the foot,’ with به marking the relation. The clause is elliptical/poetic: subject + adjectival phrase + location.
segments.s200-g23: برآشفت is a past tense/preterite verb meaning ‘grew angry/agitated’ (بر‑ prefix intensifies the verb). با خویشتن is a reflexive/inner-directed prepositional phrase ‘with himself’ (i.e., he stirred within), and چون پلنگ is a simile ‘like a leopard.’ The clause describes an internal, sudden agitation using poetic simile.
segments.s200-g24: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’ (classical for از). بافیدن here carries the literal sense ‘to weave/twist’ (used idiomatically of a limb’s movement), and پای is ‘the foot.’ آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش ‘came to him,’ so آمدش ننگ = ‘shame came upon him’ (ننگ as the nominative/theme). The construction puts the causal phrase first (ز ...) and then states the resulting experience.
segments.s200-g113: This is a nominal phrase meaning 'a certain renowned one among the Iranians.' یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/someone'; نامداری is a noun meaning 'renown/famous person' used here as the head; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and ایرانیان is the genitive (the group being referred to). Word order is indefinite word + epithet + prepositional phrase (one of the Iranians).
segments.s200-g114: بفرمود is a past, honorific reporting verb 'he commanded/ordered'; تا introduces a subordinate clause with the sense 'that/so that' and here licences a jussive/subjunctive action; تنگ modifies بندد and functions adverbially as 'tight/close'; بندد is a present-subjunctive/jussive form 'bind/close' placed at the clause end (verb-final); میان is the prepositional adverb 'between' and is the locus for the binding. Together: 'he commanded that (they) bind tightly between (them/it).'
segments.s200-g115: دهد here is a narrative/historic-present form of 'to give' used to report action ('gave' or 'brought'); شاه را is the definite object marked with را ('to the king' or 'the king (as recipient)'); آگهی is the noun 'information/news'; زین سخن is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/from this speech/these words' (زین here functions as the prepositional compound 'of this'). The unit means 'he gave the king the information about these words.'
segments.s200-g116: که introduces an indirect content clause ('that' / 'namely'); سالار لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the commander of the army' (head noun after its modifier); چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what' introducing reported speech ('what ...'); افگند is a past perfective verb 'threw/caused/put' used here in the sense of 'devised/caused to happen' (classical poetic use); بن is the noun object meaning 'foundation/root/base' used poetically as the thing affected. The clause reports 'what the army commander did/caused.'
segments.s200-g117: This is an exclamatory clause: چه روز بد آمد means 'what an ill/bad day came' or 'what a dreadful day it was'; چه is used exclamatorily, روز 'day' and بد 'bad' modify it; آمد is the verb 'came/happened.' بایرانیان indicates the people affected ('for the Iranians'). The construction is typical Persian exclamatory word order (exclamative + predicate + experiencer).
segments.s200-g118: سران را places 'the chiefs/leaders' as the topical/affected party (را marks them); ز introduces the cause or source 'from/because of'; بخشش here means 'giving/granting/distribution' (i.e. gifts or grants); سرآمد functions as 'result/conclusion' (the outcome that came about); زیان is the final noun 'loss/damage.' The phrase literally reads 'to the leaders, from the giving, the result was loss.'
segments.s200-g119: رونده ('one who goes') here means 'a rider/messenger/traveller' (subject); بر + شاه gives the direction/recipient 'to the king' (بر used concretely as 'toward/to'); برد in context functions as a verb 'brought/carried' (here the lexical senses provided don't match the verbal sense and so are marked -1); آگهی is the object 'news/information.' The clause thus means 'a rider brought news to the king.'
segments.s200-g120: که introduces the content of the news ('that...'); تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous' describing the subsequent predicate; شد is the past/becoming auxiliary 'became/was made'; آن روزگار is the temporal/epochal noun 'that time/era'; مهی is an epithet meaning 'moon‑like/beautiful' used poetically ('that moon‑like time'). The clause states that that formerly bright time turned dark.
segments.s200-g9: گرفتند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb 'they seized/took'. بسیار here is a quantifier 'many' modifying what they seized. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', and کشتند is another past verb 'they killed' (the object is implied from context). نیز after the verb adds 'also/too'.
segments.s200-g10: نبود is the negative past existential ('there was not' / 'did not exist'). از introduces the following noun phrase ('from/of'), forming the expression 'از بدبخت مانند چیز' = 'a thing like (such) misfortune'. The sequence بد بخت is a noun phrase 'bad fortune' (misfortune). مانند = 'like' and چیز = 'thing'; together the clause says that nothing of that sort of misfortune existed.
segments.s200-g11: گله‌دار و چوپان lists the victims ('herdsmen and shepherds'). بسی functions as a quantifier modifying the following predicate ('many' — here used poetically), and کشته شد is a passive/perfect expression 'were killed' (کشته = slain, شد functioning as auxiliary/change-of-state marker). The sequence conveys 'many herdsmen and shepherds were killed.'
segments.s200-g12: سر بخت is a compound nominal phrase ('سر' in the sense of 'chief/top' + 'بخت' = 'fate/fortune') meaning roughly 'the chief fortune' or 'the leading lot' of a group. ایرانیان is the genitive/possessive target ('of the Iranians'). گشته شد is a two-part verbal construction (گشته past participle of گشتن 'become/turn' + شد auxiliary) indicating a change of state: 'was turned/overturned' or 'was changed.'
segments.s200-g13: وزان جایگه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from that place' (وزان = 'and/from that'), followed by سوی ایران 'toward Iran'. سپاه ('army') is the subject; the whole chunk marks origin and direction: 'from that place the army toward Iran...'.
segments.s200-g14: برفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/off' (they set out). برسان here is a verbal infinitive/ideational purpose 'to bring/deliver' (they went in order to bring/deliver). گرد سیاه is an adjectival compound; گرد in older/poetic usage can mean 'group/host' (an archaic sense not listed in the provided senses), modified by سیاه 'black'. The phrase thus expresses purpose: 'they went to carry the news to the black host.'
segments.s200-g15: همه مست بودند is a verbal clause with predicate before subject (a poetic inversion): 'all were drunk.' بودند is the past plural of 'to be' (they were). ایرانیان names who is meant: 'the Iranians.'
segments.s200-g16: گروهی is an indefinite noun 'a group' or 'some (of them)'. نشسته is a participle/adjective 'seated'. گشاده میان is an adjectival/adverbial phrase 'wide-open in the middle' (گشاده = 'open/spacious', میان = 'between/in the middle'), describing how they were seated: 'a group sat, with a wide-open space between them in the middle.'
segments.s200-g81: همه خسته و بسته بد هرک زیست strings together a description and a short exclamation: 'همه' (subject) is followed by two adjectival/participial predicates 'خسته و بسته' (everyone was tired and bound). The sequence 'بد هرک زیست' is an archaic/poetic exclamation meaning 'woe to whoever lived' where 'بد' = 'woe/bad' and 'هرک زیست' = 'whoever lived' (هرک + زیست, past 3rd‑sg 'lived').
segments.s200-g82: شد آن کشته بر خسته باید گریست begins with the verb 'شد' (happened/it came to pass) followed by the subject 'آن کشته' ('that slain [one]'). The prepositional phrase 'بر خسته' = 'upon the weary' indicates where/whom the slain fell upon. The impersonal modal phrase 'باید گریست' ('one must weep') uses 'باید' + simple verbal form 'گریست' to express obligation; 'گریست' here functions like an infinitive/subjunctive 'to weep'.
segments.s200-g83: نه تاج و نه تخت و نه پرده‌سرای is a triple negation enumerating absent symbols of rulership and court: the repeated 'نه ... نه ... نه' = 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. The nouns follow the negative marker in coordinated list order: 'تاج' (crown), 'تخت' (throne), 'پرده‌سرای' (curtained hall/stage/inner apartments).
segments.s200-g84: نه اسپ و نه مردان جنگی بپای continues the negative list and ends with 'بپای', an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'remain/persist' (here: there remain none). The construction omits an explicit copula; the negated items 'اسپ' (steed) and 'مردان جنگی' (warlike men) are the things that 'بپای' is predicated of.
segments.s200-g85: نه آباد بوم نه مردان کار is another negative coordinate: 'آباد بوم' = 'prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد qualifying بوم) and 'مردان کار' = 'men of work' or 'able/work‑men'. The repeated 'نه' negates each noun phrase in turn.
segments.s200-g86: نه آن خستگانرا کسی خواستار places the object 'آن خستگان‑را' ('those weary ones' with the object marker -را) at the front for emphasis, followed by the subject 'کسی' and predicate 'خواستار' ('desirous of / seeking'). With the initial 'نه' the whole clause is negative: 'nor was anyone desirous of those weary people.' Note the fronted object and the typical subject–verb order 'کسی خواستار'.
segments.s200-g87: پدر بر پسر چند گریان شده is a rhetorical lament: 'پدر بر پسر' ('father over son') uses the preposition 'بر' to indicate the relation (father weeping for the son). 'چند' = 'how many' (rhetorical), and 'گریان شده' is a compound: 'گریان' (weeping) + 'شده' (become/has been) giving a perfect/resultative sense 'has been made to weep' or 'has begun to weep'.
segments.s200-g88: وزان خستگان چند بریان شده continues the lament: 'وزان' = 'and from them/and of those', 'خستگان' = 'the weary ones', 'چند' again rhetorical 'how many', and 'بریان شده' = 'have been burnt/consumed' — here used metaphorically to mean 'consumed/ruined' (not literal roasting). The clause parallels the previous one to emphasize loss.
segments.s200-g41: همی is a continuous/habitual particle meaning ‘kept / was continually’. کرد is the simple past of کردن and here functions as the main past tense verb ‘did/looked’. The subject گودرز follows the verb (poetic V–S order); هر سو نگاه is the object/phrase ‘a gaze in every direction’ with هر سو (‘every side/direction’) modifying نگاه (‘look’).
segments.s200-g42: سپاه is the subject ‘the troops/host’ and اندر آمد is an intransitive past ‘entered/came in’. بگرد here is a fossilized/prepositional sense (poetic contraction of به گرد = ‘around’) forming the adverbial phrase بگرد سپاه ‘around (them/the host)’; the clause therefore says the troops came in all around. (Note: בگرد is represented here as a contracted/archaic adverbial, not the imperative sense given in the lemma list.)
segments.s200-g43: سراسیمه شد is a two-word predicate ‘was/ became panic-stricken’ (شد = became). خفته is a past-participial/adjectival form ‘asleep’ describing the person; از introduces the cause/source (‘from/out of’), and داروگیر here means ‘the medicine‑taker’ = a patient under drugs. Together: ‘the (one) who was asleep from the medicine became panic‑struck.’
segments.s200-g44: برآمد is a past intransitive verb ‘arose/appeared’. یکی ابر is an indefinite NP ‘a certain cloud’ (یکی = ‘a certain / a’). بارانش is baran + possessive -ش ‘its rain’, and تیر follows as a nominal predicate; Persian omits the copula, so بارانش تیر reads ‘its rain (was) arrows’—a poetic way to say a shower of arrows fell like rain.
segments.s200-g45: بزیر سر is a prepositional phrase ‘under the head’; مست here qualifies the person (‘the drunk’), and بالین نرم is an NP ‘a soft pillow’. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase + qualifier + the noun describing the object).
segments.s200-g46: ز بر is an archaic/poetic combination of ز (‘from/of’) + بر (here ‘by/at/near’) giving the sense ‘at his side / beside him’. The following coordinated list names items/figures: گرز و گوپال و شمشیر گرم = ‘a mace, Gopāl, and a hot sword’. There is no overt verb; the list is understood as what lay/was beside him.
segments.s200-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. سپیده is ‘dawn’ and برزد is an archaic past verb meaning ‘rose/appeared’. سر از برج شیر is a poetic locution ‘(its) head from the Tower of the Lion’—i.e. dawn appearing over the Lion Tower—so the whole clause is ‘when dawn arose from the Tower of the Lion.’
segments.s200-g48: به لشکر نگه کرد is literally ‘made a look toward the army’: نگه (gaze/watch) plus کرد (the light verb ‘did/made’ used with noun to mean ‘looked’). The subject follows as a nominative apposition گیو دلیر = ‘Giv, the brave’. This is normal poetic word order (verb phrase before the subject).
segments.s200-g73: فرومانده is an adjectival/participial descriptor placed before the nouns (poetic word order) meaning 'exhausted/downcast'. اسپان (plural) and مردان جنگ form a coordinated noun phrase 'horses and men of war' where جنگ qualifies مردان ('warriors' / 'men of battle').
segments.s200-g74: یکی را نبد ... is an archaic negative construction: نبد (negative of بودن) = 'there was not / there lacked'. یکی را uses یکی as 'not one' (with را marking the definite/foregrounded item) so the clause means 'not one had ...'. The three nouns هوش و توش و نه هنگ are coordinated as things lacking; نه before the last element functions as the 'nor' in English.
segments.s200-g75: سپاهی is the subject ('soldiers' / 'troops'). ازین گونه is a prepositional/qualifying phrase 'of this kind' (از + این in poetic spelling), placed before the verb. گشتند is the past plural of گشتن 'turned/turned back' and باز here adverbially means 'back'/'away' (they turned back).
segments.s200-g76: شده مانده is a pair of participial/past forms: شده ('having become' / resultive) + مانده ('remaining' / 'left behind'), together indicating the resulting state 'ended up remaining / were left'. از رزم و راه دراز gives the source or cause 'from the battle and the long road' with دراز qualifying راه.
segments.s200-g77: ز هامون is a poetic-origin phrase 'from the plain' placed before the subject; سپهبد is the subject (the commander). سوی کوه شد is an idiomatic sequence: سوی + destination 'کوه' + شد (past of شدن) meaning 'turned toward / went to the mountain' — شد here serves as the verb 'turned/moved (toward)'.
segments.s200-g78: ز پیکار marks cause/origin 'from the battle'; ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. بی‌اندوه is a predicative adjective 'without-sorrow / carefree' and شد is the copular/ resultive 'became' — together 'the Turks became without sorrow (after/from the battle)'.
segments.s200-g79: فراوان here is an adverbial intensifier modifying کم آمد: 'came very few / scarcely any came'. آمد is the past tense 'came'; ز ایرانیان indicates source/whose ranks they came from 'of the Iranians'.
segments.s200-g80: برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. خروشی is the noun 'a roar/cry'; بدرد functions adjectivally/adverbially 'that causes pain' (i.e., a dreadful/painful cry). از میان is a locative 'from the midst / from among them' marking the origin of the sound.
segments.s200-g33: همی is a durative adverb here meaning ‘continually/still’. گشت is the past/telic form of گشتن meaning ‘turned/ moved (about)’ (verb before its adverbial phrase is common in poetry). بر گرد لشکر is a prepositional phrase ‘around the army’ with بر + گرد ('around/about') and the noun لشکر 'host/army'. چو دود is a simile ‘like smoke’ modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s200-g34: برانگیخت is past (3rd person) of برانگیختن ‘to rouse/raise’. آن را is a definite object (آن + را) referring back to the rising smoke; را marks that object as definite. که هشیار بود is a relative clause ‘who/which was awake/alert’ (که = who/which; هشیار = awake/alert; بود = past ‘was’), so the whole clause means ‘it roused those who were alert’.
segments.s200-g35: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘someone/a certain one’. جنگ با بیژن is the object phrase ‘a fight with Bijan’ marked by با ‘with’. افگند is the past of افکندن ‘to cast/launch’ used here as ‘launched/declared’; پی follows the verb as an adverbial ‘in pursuit/after’, so the clause says ‘someone set a fight against Bijan and pursued (him)’.
segments.s200-g36: که here introduces an explanatory clause ‘for/since’. این دشت رزم است states the predication ‘this plain is a battlefield’ (رزم = battle; است = is). است‌گر is the fused conditional/particle meaning ‘if / should it be’ used rhetorically here to contrast the plain’s nature (classical usage: ‘if it is a battlefield (not a… )’). باغ می (‘garden [of] wine’) contrasts a peaceful garden with a battlefield; می is ‘wine’ (not the mi‑prefix).
segments.s200-g37: وزان پس is a temporal/continuative phrase ‘from that (time) then/after that’. بیامد is the past form ‘came’. سوی کارزار is the directional complement ‘toward the battlefield’ (سوی = toward, کارزار = battlefield). The sequence places the time/adverbial before the verb as is common in narrative.
segments.s200-g38: برشتابید is a verbal form meaning ‘hurried/hastened’ (here past tense ‘they hastened’). چندی سوار is the subject phrase ‘several/some horsemen’; note the poetic inversion where the verb phrase precedes its subject. بره appears here as a short adverbial/particle ('forward' or similar) in the clause; its exact classical function is archaic/poetic and does not match the provided noun senses.
segments.s200-g39: بدان is a compound adverb meaning ‘at that / with that’ (here marking immediate consequence). اندکی qualifies the following noun as ‘a little / briefly / a small number’. برکشیدند is past plural ‘they drew up/raised’ (here ‘drew a line’ or ‘formed’). نخ here means ‘line/string’ (نخ سپاهی = a line/cord of soldiers). The phrase describes a brief formation or movement of the troops.
segments.s200-g40: سپاهی is a collective noun ‘a troop/army unit’. ز ترکان means ‘of/from the Turks’ (ز = from/of in poetic register; ترکان = the Turks — here the enemy contingent). چو مور و ملخ is a simile ‘like ants and locusts’, comparing their numbers and movement to swarming insects.
segments.s200-g1: گزین کرد is a compound past tense: گزین (choose) + کرد (did) = 'chose'. زان is the poetic preposition 'from that', and لشکر نامدار is a genitival noun phrase 'that renowned host/army'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: verb first, then the source phrase 'from that renowned army'.
segments.s200-g2: This is a noun phrase giving what was chosen: سواران شمشیرزن سی‌هزار = 'thirty thousand mounted swordsmen'. شمشیرزن is an attributive compound noun 'sword-wielder' modifying سواران, and the numeral سی‌هزار follows the nouns (a common poetic/inverted order) to quantify them.
segments.s200-g3: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/they went'. نیمی گذشته ز شب is a temporal clause 'half (of it) having passed of the night' = 'when half the night had passed' (گذشته ز + noun = 'having passed of X'). The phrase modifies the time of برفتند.
segments.s200-g4: نه ... نه ... is correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. بانگ تبیره functions as a compound 'the cry (banḡ) called/tabireh' (a watch- or alarm-cry), and بوق و جلب coordinates 'horn and (general) clamor/noise'. Each noun is negated by the preceding نه.
segments.s200-g5: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیران سالار in this context is a titular name/title (Piran Salār) functioning as the subject; although پیران can mean 'elders', here it is the proper-name/title. لشکر is the direct object 'the host/army' and براند is the third-person singular verbal form 'drove/put into motion' (راندن – 'to drive'), used narratively.
segments.s200-g6: میان یلان is a locative 'between the heroes' (میان = 'between', یلان = 'heroes'), هفت فرسنگ is the distance 'seven farsangs', and ماند is the past/resultative 'remained/lay' — so the clause says that seven farsangs remained between the champions.
segments.s200-g7: نخستین is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'first' (here adverbially). رسیدند is past plural 'they arrived'. پیش گله = 'in front of / before the flock' (پیش = 'before/in front of', گله = 'flock/herd'), so the clause means 'first they arrived before the flock'.
segments.s200-g8: کجا بود is the interrogative 'where was ...'. بر دشت توران is the locative phrase 'on the plain of Turan' (بر = 'on/upon', دشت = 'plain', توران = 'Turan'). یله here functions as a proper name (the person Yeleh) rather than the adjective 'loose', so its lexical senses do not apply and it is marked -1.

sentences.s200-s45: Such is the world's way: the world keeps its own deeds hidden from you.
sentences.s200-s46: It continually plays with you behind a veil, and outwardly makes you seem self-sufficient.
sentences.s200-s47: You came from the wind; you will go (back) to the dust — what do you know of what they will do to you?
sentences.s200-s48: We close up and lie stretched out, and in the grasp of greed we no longer openly disclose the secret.
sentences.s200-s33: Thus came this swift-turning vault; sometimes it brings joy, at other times pain.
sentences.s200-s34: The Turanian horsemen were at Tus's rear; hearts full of vengeance and chiefs full of whispering counsel.
sentences.s200-s35: Maces rained down continually, as if from a cloud; from the rear upon cuirass, upon the man himself, and upon the infidel.
sentences.s200-s36: No one inside the field remained steadfast; they made every mountain into a circling rampart.
sentences.s200-s25: He saw the whole plain strewn with slain Iranians; he saw that the head of Fate had turned and awakened.
sentences.s200-s26: The banner was torn and the drum lamenting.
sentences.s200-s27: The spahbed looked around, and he saw no troop; he did not see any of the host of the brave and the men.
sentences.s200-s28: Throughout the battlefield all had been slain; their bodies were soaked in blood.
sentences.s200-s61: When the brave king heard these words, he boiled over, and the sorrow was lifted from his heart.
sentences.s200-s62: He was full of grief because of his brother's affairs; that grief — sorrow upon sorrow — the host increased.
sentences.s200-s63: He spoke, as if saying 'Curse Tus!'; the dark night lasted until the rooster's cry.
sentences.s200-s29: The son became fatherless, the father became sonless; the whole army, hungry, was turned upside-down.
sentences.s200-s30: In their misery they turned their faces back; they set up inner curtains and tents.
sentences.s200-s31: Neither drum nor army, neither baggage nor bundles; all were without provisions, weary and on the right (bank).
sentences.s200-s32: Thus the host went toward the Kaseh River, penniless and stripped even of warp and weft.
sentences.s200-s13: He came on horseback and set his foot inside; with stately bearing he came forth from his place.
sentences.s200-s14: He reached the spahbed's inner chamber; from the dust of the host he saw the sky darkened.
sentences.s200-s15: He said to him, 'Stand up — an army is coming.' Then a cloud of dust rose from the place of arrival.
sentences.s200-s16: From that spot he went to his father and put the bull‑headed mace into his hand.
sentences.s200-s49: Two of the Iranians had been killed; another had returned, weary from battle.
sentences.s200-s50: The spahbed, maddened by the fighting, became insane; his heart, toward wisdom, grew like a stranger.
sentences.s200-s51: In the army-camp, amid wine, table and revelry, the host longed to be again on the field of battle.
sentences.s200-s52: World‑wise Goudarz, with his hoary head, had neither son nor descendant, neither homeland nor kin.
sentences.s200-s53: Neither that food for the weary nor any physician (could help); sorrow was everywhere and his tears were blood‑stained.
sentences.s200-s54: The experienced (men) came before him; they came broken‑hearted and seeking the way.
sentences.s200-s55: A certain lookout went up on the mountain top; to see where, he turned his eyes toward the mass (the crowd).
sentences.s200-s56: He sent out the vanguard to every direction, in hopes that he might find a remedy for that affliction.
sentences.s200-s9: Inside the tent Giv was awake; the commander Goudarz was alert.
sentences.s200-s10: A roar arose and the cry of the axe's wound; Giv became frantic and aggressive.
sentences.s200-s11: A cloud stood before the curtain-stage; a certain mighty steed at the foot.
sentences.s200-s12: He stirred within himself like a leopard; from the twisting/weaving of the foot shame came upon him.
sentences.s200-s57: A certain renowned man of the Iranians commanded that they bind it tightly in between.
sentences.s200-s58: He gave the king information of this speech — (telling) what the army's commander had devised.
sentences.s200-s59: What a bad day befell the Iranians; to the chiefs, loss was the result of the gifts.
sentences.s200-s60: A rider brought news to the king that that moon‑like time had grown dark.
sentences.s200-s5: They seized many and also killed others; there was nothing like such misfortune.
sentences.s200-s6: Many herdsmen and shepherds were killed; the Iranians' fortune was overturned.
sentences.s200-s7: From that place the army set off toward Iran; they went to carry the news to the black host.
sentences.s200-s8: All the Iranians were drunk; a group sat with wide-open space between them in the middle.
sentences.s200-s41: All were weary and bound; woe to whoever lived—when that slain man fell upon the weary one, one must weep.
sentences.s200-s42: Neither crown nor throne nor curtained palace; neither steed nor warlike men remained.
sentences.s200-s43: No prosperous land, no working men; nor was there anyone desirous of those weary ones.
sentences.s200-s44: How many a father has been made to weep for his son, and how many of those weary ones have been consumed!
sentences.s200-s21: Goudarz kept looking in every direction as troops came in all around.
sentences.s200-s22: The drugged sleeper was thrown into panic and rose up; a cloud appeared whose rain was arrows.
sentences.s200-s23: Beneath the drunk's head was a soft pillow; at his side were a mace, Gopāl, and a hot sword.
sentences.s200-s24: When dawn rose from the Tower of the Lion, Giv the brave turned his gaze to the army.
sentences.s200-s37: The exhausted horses and warriors — not one had any wit, any strength, nor even a moment (of alertness).
sentences.s200-s38: Soldiers of this sort turned back; they remained, left behind by the battle and by the long road.
sentences.s200-s39: From the plain the spahbed turned toward the mountain; from the fight the Turks became without sorrow.
sentences.s200-s40: Very few of the Iranians came; from among them there rose a painful cry.
sentences.s200-s17: He kept moving about the host like smoke; when smoke rose it roused those who were alert.
sentences.s200-s18: Someone launched a fight against Bijan and gave chase, for this plain is a battlefield, not a garden of wine.
sentences.s200-s19: After that he came toward the battlefield; several horsemen hastened forward.
sentences.s200-s20: At that (moment) they drew up a short line; a troop of Turks swarmed like ants and locusts.
sentences.s200-s1: He chose thirty thousand mounted swordsmen from that renowned host.
sentences.s200-s2: They departed when half the night had passed; there was neither the cry of ‘tabireh’ nor horn or clamor.
sentences.s200-s3: When Piran, the venerable commander, drove the host forward, seven farsangs lay between the heroes.
sentences.s200-s4: First they came before the flock; where was Yeleh on the plain of Turan?
